summary: Early modern English philosopher, often credited as the father of the scientific method. Author of Novum Organum, The Advancement of Learning, and the utopian novel The New Atlantis. Outlined the doctrine of the four idols of the mind and championed inductive reasoning over Aristotelian syllogism. Proposed the Great Instauration as a sixfold reformation of knowledge.

works.name: Novum Organum and the Scientific Method
works.description: Bacon's most frequently clued work, the Novum Organum (1620), was intended as part of a larger project called the Great Instauration (Instauratio Magna). The title references Aristotle's Organon, the collected works on logic, and Bacon's aim was to replace Aristotelian syllogistic logic with his method of 'true induction' or 'eliminative induction.' The work is best known for its doctrine of the four idols and its systematic method of arranging data into tables of presence, absence, and degrees to investigate natural phenomena like heat.

clues.clue: four idols of the mind: Tribe, Cave, Marketplace, and Theatre — sources of false reasoning and mental defects
examples: outlined 'empty dogmas' in the form of four idols of the mind
labeled misconceptions founded in human nature and through men's mutual association as the idols of the tribe and of the marketplace

clues.clue: championed 'true induction' over Aristotelian syllogisms; aimed to replace syllogistic logic with empirical experimentation
examples: championed 'true induction' over syllogisms, which he claimed were not a method of acquiring new knowledge
sought to replace Aristotelian logic with his namesake method of 'eliminative induction'

clues.clue: title references Aristotle's Organon — named as a 'new' instrument of science
examples: expanded on the syllogistic method in a work whose title references Aristotle's Organon
named after Aristotle's collected works on logic

clues.clue: tables of presentation / presence, absence, and degrees — method for organizing data from the senses to investigate natural phenomena like heat
examples: divided data into tables of presence, essence, and degrees
compared a 'table of existence and presence' and a 'table of deviation' as part of his 'First Vintage of the Form of Heat'

clues.clue: heat described as 'motion and nothing else' — among the first to describe heat as a form of motion
examples: asserts that heat is 'motion and nothing else'
was among the first to describe heat as a form of motion

clues.clue: preface lauds ancient Greeks for middle position between skepticism and certainty; sets out to 'establish degrees of certainty'
examples: lauds the ancient Greeks for taking a middle position between total skepticism and complete certainty

clues.clue: 'prerogative instances' including 'Instances of the Fingerpost' — 27 kinds; cited by John R. Platt as inspiration for 'Strong Inference'
examples: listed 27 kinds of 'prerogative instances' in which one can employ the 'eliminative' form of a method
John R. Platt cited this thinker's use of 'prerogative instances' like 'Instances of the Fingerpost'

clues.clue: frontispiece shows a ship passing through columns below two globes with outstretched hands
examples: frontispiece of a text by this man shows a ship passing through a pair of columns below two globes with outstretched hands

works.name: The Great Instauration (Instauratio Magna)
works.description: Bacon's planned magnum opus, a sixfold reformation of all human knowledge, of which only the second part (the Novum Organum) was ever completed. The project aimed to reform knowledge in six parts, likened to God's creation of the world in six days. It included a 'Ladder of the Intellect' and a 'Formula of Interpretation.' The Advancement of Learning became the first volume after revision.

clues.clue: sixfold reformation of knowledge called the 'Great Instauration' (Instauratio Magna) — only the second part (Novum Organum) was completed
examples: proposed a sixfold reformation of knowledge termed the 'Great Instauration'
aimed to reform all of the knowledge known to man in six parts, akin to how God created the world in six days

clues.clue: 'Ladder of the Intellect' and 'Formula of Interpretation' — illustrative devices within the Great Instauration
examples: illustrated his 'Formula of Interpretation' with a 'Ladder of the Intellect'

clues.clue: 'first vintages' — primary approximations to the truth, applied to concept of 'potential heat'
examples: described primary approximations to the truth as 'first vintages,' which he applied to his concept of 'potential heat'

works.name: The Advancement of Learning
works.description: Published in 1605, this work classifies human knowledge into the categories of history, poesy, and philosophy. It describes three 'distempers' of false learning — the delicate, the contentious, and the fantastical — and distinguishes between 'pure' and 'proud' knowledge. It inspired the 'system of human knowledge' in Diderot's Encyclopedie. A revised version became the first volume of the Great Instauration.

clues.clue: three forms of false learning: 'delicate,' 'contentious,' and 'fantastical' — categorized as 'distempers' that impede academic progress
examples: described three forms of false learning as 'delicate', 'contentious', and 'fantastical'
discusses the fantastical, contentious and delicate forms of the title concept, which he refers to as 'distempers'

clues.clue: classified knowledge into history, poesy, and philosophy; inspired the 'system of human knowledge' in Diderot's Encyclopedie
examples: advocated reclassifying knowledge into the three categories of history, poesy, and philosophy
The 'system of human knowledge' in Diderot's Encyclopedie was inspired by this thinker's The Advancement of Learning

clues.clue: distinguished between 'pure' and 'proud' knowledge
examples: distinguished between 'pure' and 'proud' knowledge in his work The Advancement of Learning

works.name: The New Atlantis and Salomon's House
works.description: Bacon's utopian novel (1627) set on the fictional island of Bensalem, featuring the ideal research institute called Salomon's House. The institution is 'dedicated to the study of the works and creatures of God' and includes 'Merchants of Light' who travel the world gathering knowledge and 'Inoculaters' who execute experiments. It represents Bacon's vision of organized scientific inquiry.

clues.clue: utopian novel set on the island of Bensalem featuring Salomon's House, the ideal research institute
examples: imagined a utopian university called Salomon's House
wrote a novel set on the island of Bensalem that features Salomon's House, the ideal research institute

clues.clue: Salomon's House includes 'Merchants of Light' who gather knowledge and 'Inoculaters' who execute experiments devised by 'Lamps'
examples: Members of this institution include 'Merchants of Light' who travel around the world gathering knowledge and 'Inoculaters' who execute experiments devised by members called 'Lamps'

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Common giveaway identifiers for Francis Bacon include his role as an English philosopher of the early modern period who formulated the scientific method, his association with the Novum Organum, and his being called the 'father of experimental philosophy.' Biographical clues include the famous story of his death from stuffing a chicken with snow to test food preservation, his cosmological ideas involving quaternions of mercury and sulfur, and his classification of magic as applied science.

clues.clue: English philosopher who formulated a version of the scientific method in Novum Organum — common giveaway
examples: name this English philosopher who wrote Novum Organum and spearheaded the Scientific Revolution
name this advocate of the scientific method who wrote Novum Organum

clues.clue: called the 'father of experimental philosophy' by Voltaire (twelfth Philosophical Letter)
examples: The twelfth of Voltaire's Philosophical Letters praises this 'father of experimental philosophy'

clues.clue: stuffed a plucked chicken with snow to test food preservation, creating the first frozen chicken — died from this experiment
examples: after having an argument with Dr. Winterbourne about food preservation, this English philosopher stuffed a plucked chicken full of snow

clues.clue: bee analogy — contrasted 'empirics' (ants) and 'dogmatical' thinkers (spiders) with the 'true labor of philosophy' (bee)
examples: analogizes those who are 'empirics' or 'dogmatical' to ants and spiders, but likens the 'true labor of philosophy' to a bee

clues.clue: cosmology divided into quaternions of mercury and sulfur; classified magic as applied science
examples: divided his cosmology into 'two tribes' of quaternions consisting of mercury and sulfur
classified magic as applied science and categorized matter into twelve segments

clues.clue: printing, gunpowder, and compass as symbols of personal, national, and human ambition
examples: uses printing, gunpowder, and the compass as symbols of personal ambition, national ambition, and the ambition of the human race

clues.clue: dubbed Telesio 'the first of the moderns' for his focus on sense-perception
examples: dubbed Telesio the 'first of the moderns' for his focus on sense-perception

clues.clue: rejected magic due to emphasis on individual skill and gains; promoted empirical science instead
examples: rejected the use of magic because of its emphasis on individual skill and individual gains

clues.clue: essay declares 'Prosperity is the blessing of the Old Testament; Adversity is the blessing of the New'
examples: One essay by this author declares that 'Prosperity is the blessing of the Old Testament; Adversity is the blessing of the New'

clues.clue: shares surname with Roger Bacon (author of Opus Maius, medieval empiricist)
examples: shares this surname with a philosopher who only wrote four parts of his Instauratio Magna

comprehensive_summary:
Francis Bacon (1561-1626) is one of the most consistently clued early modern philosophers in quizbowl, appearing primarily as the architect of the scientific method and the author of the Novum Organum. The overwhelming majority of clues center on three topics: the four idols of the mind, the method of induction, and the relationship between Novum Organum and Aristotle's Organon.

The four idols are the single most common clue, appearing in nearly every tossup and many bonuses. They are the Idols of the Tribe (rooted in human nature), the Cave (individual faults and biases), the Marketplace (abuse of language and mutual association), and the Theatre (mindless adherence to received philosophical systems, likened to stage plays). Bacon's championing of 'true induction' over Aristotelian syllogistic logic is the second most common clue and frequently serves as the giveaway. The title Novum Organum itself references Aristotle's Organon.

Bacon's empirical method is described through his use of 'tables of presentation' — tables of presence, absence, and degrees — to systematically investigate phenomena like heat. He described heat as 'motion and nothing else,' making him one of the first to propose a kinetic theory of heat. His 'First Vintage of the Form of Heat' is a specific application of this method. Related power clues include his 27 kinds of 'prerogative instances' (cited by John R. Platt as inspiration for 'Strong Inference') and his concept of 'first vintages' as preliminary approximations to truth.

The Great Instauration (Instauratio Magna) was Bacon's planned sixfold reformation of all knowledge, of which only the Novum Organum was completed. The Advancement of Learning, which became its first volume, classifies knowledge into history, poesy, and philosophy, and identifies three 'distempers' of false learning: the delicate, contentious, and fantastical. It inspired the classification system used in Diderot's Encyclopedie.

The New Atlantis, Bacon's utopian novel set on the island of Bensalem, features Salomon's House, an idealized research institution with specialized roles including 'Merchants of Light' and 'Inoculaters.' This frequently appears in bonus questions about utopian literature alongside Thomas More's Utopia and Campanella's City of the Sun.

Biographical clues include the famous story of Bacon stuffing a chicken with snow to test food preservation (leading to his death), his bee-versus-ant-versus-spider analogy for empiricism, and his cosmological ideas involving quaternions of mercury and sulfur. He is often called the 'father of experimental philosophy,' a title given by Voltaire in his Philosophical Letters. Bacon shares his surname with the medieval philosopher Roger Bacon, which occasionally appears in dual-answer tossups.
